straight_beat.name: Helmut Raskin
straight_beat.column:
The Brewers lost again, but not without a fight. The Blue Jays didn’t just beat them—they beat them with a combination of precision, patience, and a bullpen that looked like it had been handed a playbook written by a chess grandmaster. The box score is loaf-shaped, but that’s not the point. The point is that the Brewers’ offense, which has been a bright spot all season, found itself in a pickle yesterday.  

Yelich doubled twice, Woodruff went seven, and the Brewers took the rubber game from the Cubs. Divisional lead now sits at four. Wait—no, that was last week. Yesterday, they lost to the Blue Jays. The numbers don’t lie: 9-7. But here’s the thing. The Brewers scored seven runs, yet still lost. That’s not a fluke. It’s a pattern. They’ve been doing this all year. You can’t hit a home run and then watch the ball get caught in the warning track. You can’t load the bases and then let a reliever throw a pitch that gets hit for a grand slam. You can’t have a pitcher who’s been lights-out in April and then have him struggle in May.  

Let’s talk about the bullpen. Woodruff’s seven innings were solid, but the real story was the relief corps. The Blue Jays’ offense didn’t just capitalize on mistakes—they exploited them. The Brewers’ relievers walked three batters, surrendered four runs, and looked like they were trying to throw a knuckleball in a hurricane. The front office has been talking about “building depth,” but depth doesn’t mean anything if the guys in the pen can’t command their

optimist.name: Tippy Kowalczyk
optimist.column:
The Brewers lost yesterday, sure. But let’s not let that one game cloud the fact that this team is still got the heart of a thousand bratwursts. You know what’s funny? The way the Blue Jays’ bats woke up like a kid who finally got a new video game. Nine runs? That’s not a loss—it’s a wake-up call for the opposition. And hey, Milwaukee’s got a 8-8 record? That’s not a slide, that’s a rollercoaster ride with a view of the horizon.  

Now, let’s talk about the Brewers’ offense. They’re not perfect, but they’re got the kind of talent that makes you want to stick around. Look at those young sluggers—Koechlin, that rookie, he’s got the swing of

pessimist.name: Dick Szczepanski
pessimist.column:
Another September fade in April. Mark Attanasio’s running a small-market charity and we’re the patrons. Get me another beer.  

The Brewers’ 8-8 record isn’t just a statistical blip—it’s a narrative. A story written in red ink, inked by the front office’s inability to build a team that can string wins together. Yesterday’s 9-7 loss to the Blue Jays wasn’t just another stumble; it was a familiar script. The same old story
